anchorText: as if she ranked My gift of a nine-hundred-years-old name35 With anybody’s gift

levels.body: The theme of jealousy runs constantly throughout the poem. Starting from lines 13-15, the duke’s tone becomes cynical when he claims that he was not the only one who brought joy to the duchess. Immediately afterwards, Fra Pandolf’s role as her painter is also put into question by the duke when the duke suspects him of flirtation (cf. Level 1 Context [[fra-pandolf]]). Lines 25-30 list the different events and actions along with the duke’s own favour, that made the duchess smile. These prompts clearly seemed frivolous and unworthy of gratitude to the duke, especially in comparison to his immaterial “gift of a nine-hundred-years-old name” (Browning, l. 33), and “they reveal his tone of barely-controlled anger” (Watson 72). The fact that the duchess set others’ behaviours on par with the duke’s was “intolerable” (Adler 222) to him, and he is found “consumed by envy of his own inferiors – even of his duchess’ mule” (Adler 222).
worksCited: Adler, Joshua. “Structure and Meaning in Browning’s ‘My Last Duchess.’” *Victorian Poetry*, vol. 15, no. 3, 1977, pp. 219–27. *JSTOR*, www.jstor.org/stable/40002116. Accessed 5 Jul. 2022.
Browning, Robert. “My Last Duchess by Robert Browning.” *Poetry Foundation*, www.poetryfoundation.org/poems/43768/my-last-duchess. Accessed 10 Aug. 2022
Watson, J. R. “Robert Browning: ‘My Last Duchess.’” *Critical Survey*, vol. 6, no. 1/2, 1973, pp. 69–75. *JSTOR*, www.jstor.org/stable/41553914. Accessed 7 Jul. 2022.

levels.body: If we assume that Browning’s duke is based on Alfonso II (cf. Level 1 Context [[ferrara]]), then we understand that he came from an extremely well-established family. Marriages among the wealthy were a serious affair, as they controlled the political and social status of both the families involved (Herlihy 7). Because the duke gifted the duchess his 900-year-old name through marriage, and thereby a rise in society with wealth and a higher social status, he expects her to value his prestigious name and appreciate his “inestimable gift” (Friedland 679) to her and her family much more than simple flatteries by acquaintances.
worksCited: Friedland, Louis S. “Ferrara and ‘My Last Duchess.’” *Studies in Philology*, vol. 33, no. 4, University of North Carolina Press, 1936, pp. 656–84,* JSTOR*, www.jstor.org/stable/4172343. Accessed 11 Jul. 2022.
Herlihy, David. "The Family in Renaissance Italy." T*he Forum Series, vol. Forum Press*, 1974, pp. 1-14, courses.washington.edu/hsteu401/Herlihy%20Family.PDF. Accessed 10 Aug. 2022.

levels.body:
The duke carries with him a nine-hundred-year-old legacy that he considers a gift which he expects to be valued above all else. In lines 43-44, the duchess’ smile is referred to more as an accusation, rather than something pleasant. The speaker suggests that her approval has lost its value because it is given ever so freely to anybody (l. 30). It is this indiscrimination that fuels the duke’s jealousy and finally pushes him over the edge, leading him to “give commands” [cf. Level 2 Interpretation [[i-gave-commands-then-all-smiles-stopped-together]]) for all smiles to stop. 
Elizabeth Gemmette quotes Joseph Solimine who notes that, “when the Duke’s jealousy encompasses a mule, when he can allow himself to become unsettled over his wife's devotion to her pet, then it can be inferred that the Duke’s hold upon reality is tenuous indeed" (Gemmette 42). After having gifted the duchess his name and all the luxuries that came with it, he then takes it all away again as a punishment by murdering her, thinking her too little grateful for his gift. His jealousy and self-importance, following this line of thought, goes beyond any rational reasoning, which eventually turns him into a cruel murderer.
worksCited: Gemmette, Elizabeth V. “Browning's ‘My Last Duchess’: An Untenable Position.” *Studies in Browning and His Circle*, vol. 10, no. 1, 1982, pp. 40–45. *JSTOR*, www.jstor.org/stable/45285104. Accessed 11 Jul. 2022.
